name: Shrutakarma
description: Shrutakarma is a significant figure in Hindu mythology, primarily known as the son of Sahadeva, one of the Pandava brothers in the epic Mahabharata, and his wife, Vijaya. He was a valiant warrior, embodying the qualities of courage, skill, and loyalty characteristic of the Pandava lineage. Shrutakarma played an active role in the Kurukshetra War, fighting on the side of the Pandavas against the Kauravas. While not as prominently featured as some of the other major heroes, his contributions to the Pandava victory were undoubtedly valuable. He was known for his prowess with various weapons, particularly the mace and the sword, and his strategic acumen on the battlefield. His lineage and association with Sahadeva, known for his wisdom and justice, further highlight Shrutakarma's virtuous character. He met his end during the Kurukshetra War, displaying unwavering bravery until his final moments. Shrutakarma's life serves as an example of dharma and dedication to one's duty, even in the face of overwhelming odds. His story, though not as widely recounted, is an integral part of the larger narrative of the Mahabharata, enriching the epic's tapestry of heroism and sacrifice.
subtitle: Valiant son of Sahadeva.
achievements: Fought valiantly in the Kurukshetra War
Demonstrated skill in wielding various weapons
Upheld Dharma and loyalty to the Pandava cause
Displayed courage in the face of death

keyMyths.title: Birth and Lineage
keyMyths.description: Shrutakarma was born to Sahadeva and his wife, Vijaya. He belonged to the Pandava lineage, inheriting their values of righteousness and duty.

keyMyths.title: Role in Kurukshetra War
keyMyths.description: Shrutakarma actively participated in the Kurukshetra War, fighting alongside his father and uncles. He contributed to the Pandava effort, displaying skill and bravery on the battlefield.

keyMyths.title: Death in Battle
keyMyths.description: Shrutakarma met his end during the Kurukshetra War, fighting valiantly against the Kauravas. His death is a testament to his dedication to dharma and his unwavering loyalty.

sources.author: Vyasa
sources.title: Mahabharata
sources.description: The primary source for information on Shrutakarma, detailing his lineage and participation in the Kurukshetra War.

sources.author: John Dowson

worshipPractices.name: Ancestor Veneration
worshipPractices.description: While not directly worshipped, Shrutakarma is honored through the veneration of his father, Sahadeva, and the Pandava lineage, who are revered for their adherence to dharma and righteousness. Recitation of the Mahabharata serves as a form of respect.

cultural.modernLegacy: Shrutakarma's character serves as a reminder of the importance of upholding dharma and familial duty, particularly in times of conflict. He is often referenced in discussions about the Mahabharata, especially when analyzing the younger generation of warriors involved in the Kurukshetra War. His story reinforces the values of courage, loyalty, and adherence to one's principles.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Training
extendedContent.content: Shrutakarma, the son of Sahadeva and Vijaya, was born into a world steeped in the traditions of the Pandava dynasty. His early life was characterized by rigorous training in martial arts, weaponry, and the principles of dharma. Growing up under the tutelage of his father and other skilled instructors, he quickly developed into a formidable warrior, embodying the qualities of courage, discipline, and strategic thinking. From a young age, he was instilled with a strong sense of justice and a commitment to upholding righteousness, values that would guide him throughout his life. His upbringing within the Pandava household exposed him to the intricacies of political intrigue and the responsibilities of leadership, preparing him for the challenges that lay ahead. Shrutakarma’s early life was thus a crucible, forging him into a warrior ready to face the trials of the Kurukshetra War.

extendedContent.title: Role in the Kurukshetra War
extendedContent.content: During the Kurukshetra War, Shrutakarma fought valiantly on the side of the Pandavas, demonstrating his prowess as a skilled archer and warrior. He engaged in numerous battles, displaying unwavering courage and determination in the face of adversity. Although not as prominently featured as some of the other major warriors, his contributions were significant in bolstering the Pandava forces. He fought against notable Kaurava warriors, holding his own and contributing to the overall war effort. Shrutakarma’s loyalty to his father and the Pandava cause was unwavering, and he consistently placed himself in harm's way to protect his comrades and uphold dharma. His participation in the war showcased his commitment to justice and his willingness to fight for what he believed in, solidifying his reputation as a true Pandava warrior. Details of specific battles are limited in the primary texts, but his presence signifies the continuation of the Pandava legacy.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Significance
extendedContent.content: Shrutakarma's legacy lies primarily in his embodiment of the Pandava ideals of courage, loyalty, and adherence to dharma. While his individual exploits may not be as widely celebrated as those of Arjuna or Bhima, his character serves as an important reminder of the collective strength and unwavering commitment of the Pandava lineage. He represents the younger generation of warriors who stepped up to defend righteousness during the Kurukshetra War, carrying the torch of their fathers and upholding the principles of justice and integrity. Shrutakarma's story reinforces the importance of familial duty and the responsibility to stand up for what is right, even in the face of overwhelming odds. His life, though relatively brief, serves as an inspiration to those who strive to embody the virtues of courage, loyalty, and unwavering commitment to dharma. He is a symbol of the enduring strength and resilience of the Pandava legacy.

shortDescription: Shrutakarma, son of Sahadeva, was a valiant warrior in the Kurukshetra War, embodying Pandava virtues of courage and loyalty. He represents the next generation upholding dharma.
